meta.name: Daily Reflection Tree
meta.author: DT Fellowship Candidate
meta.description: An end-of-day deterministic reflection tool based on Locus of Control (Rotter, 1954), Organizational Citizenship Behavior (Organ, 1988), and Self-Transcendence (Maslow, 1969).

nodes.text: Good evening. You made it to the end of the day. Before you close this chapter, let's sit with it for a few minutes — not to judge it, just to understand it.
If today were a weather report, what would it be?
options: Stormy — things went sideways
Overcast — low energy, just got through it
Partly cloudy — mixed bag
Clear — things flowed

options.condition: answer=Stormy — things went sideways|Overcast — low energy, just got through it
answer=Partly cloudy — mixed bag|Clear — things flowed

nodes.text: You said it felt '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When the day pushed back, what was your first honest instinct?
options: I looked for what I could still control
I felt blocked by things or people outside me
I pushed through on willpower alone
I froze and waited for clarity

nodes.text: You said it felt '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. When something worked well today, what would you attribute it to?
options: I prepared for it or adapted deliberately
The team / environment set me up for it
Honestly, it was a lucky break
I made a call that paid off

options.condition: answer=I looked for what I could still control|I pushed through on willpower alone
answer=I felt blocked by things or people outside me|I froze and waited for clarity
answer=I prepared for it or adapted deliberately|I made a call that paid off
answer=The team / environment set me up for it|Honestly, it was a lucky break

nodes.text: There was a moment today where you had a choice — maybe a small one. What did that look like?
options: I chose how I responded to someone's difficult behaviour
I chose to keep going when it would've been easier to stop
I chose to speak up instead of staying quiet
I chose to ask for help rather than struggle alone

nodes.text: When you give credit to luck or the team, is that the full story?
options: Mostly — I genuinely think the situation helped me
Partly — I also prepared, but I don't want to overstate it
Not really — I made choices that got me here
I'm not sure — I haven't thought about it that way

nodes.text: When you felt blocked today, was there any moment — however small — where you still had a choice?
options: Yes — and I used it
Yes — but I didn't act on it
Not really, the constraint was real
I'm not sure — I was too deep in it to notice

options.condition: always
answer=Yes — and I used it|Yes — but I didn't act on it
answer=Not really, the constraint was real|I'm not sure — I was too deep in it to notice
always

nodes.text: You stayed in the driver's seat today — even if it didn't feel that way. Recognising your choices, especially the small ones, is not self-congratulation. It's accuracy. That's how agency compounds over time.
You can see the choice — that's already more than most people do on a hard day. Seeing it and not taking it is information too. What was in the way?

Some constraints are genuinely real. But the interesting question is not whether the wall was there — it's what you did at the wall. Even the decision to wait is a decision. Worth holding that thought as we move on.
You've looked at how you handled today. Now let's look at what you gave.
Think of the most energy-consuming part of your day. How did you show up in it?
options: I gave more than was expected of me
I did what was required — fair exchange
I felt I wasn't getting enough back for what I gave
I pulled back — I didn't have it in me today

options.condition: answer=I gave more than was expected of me|I did what was required — fair exchange
answer=I felt I wasn't getting enough back for what I gave|I pulled back — I didn't have it in me today

nodes.text: Was there a moment today you did something for someone else — not because you had to, but because you could?
options: Yes — I helped a colleague with something not in my job description
Yes — I shared knowledge or feedback without being asked
Sort of — I made something easier for the team
Not really today

nodes.text: When you think about what you deserved to receive today — recognition, help, a fair workload — what comes up?
options: I expected more acknowledgment than I got
I felt others got credit for work I contributed to
I wanted more support but didn't ask for it
I'm not sure what I expected — I just felt off

options.condition: answer=Yes — I helped a colleague with something not in my job description|Yes — I shared knowledge or feedback without being asked|Sort of — I made something easier for the team
answer=Not really today
always

nodes.text: Giving without being asked is easy to overlook in your own accounting of the day. But it compounds — both in what you build and in who you become. You chose to invest in someone else's day. That's not small.
Some days the tank is just empty. That's honest, and honest beats performative. What would it look like tomorrow to give one small thing — not because you owe it, but because it's available?

Feeling underrecognised is real data — not a character flaw. But there's a useful question buried in it: when you were looking for what you deserved, were you also looking for what you could give? Both can be true at once. Tomorrow is a new ledger.
You've looked at what you gave. Now let's look at who you were thinking about.
When you think about today's most difficult moment, whose experience was in your mind?
options: Mine — I was focused on getting through it
Me and a specific colleague who was also affected
The whole team — this impacted us collectively
Someone downstream — the end user or someone we serve

options.condition: answer=Mine — I was focused on getting through it
answer=Me and a specific colleague who was also affected|The whole team — this impacted us collectively
answer=Someone downstream — the end user or someone we serve

nodes.text: Staying focused on your own experience isn't wrong — sometimes it's necessary. But was there anyone else affected by what you were going through today?
options: Probably yes, but I didn't have bandwidth to notice
Yes — and I did check in with them
I don't think so — this was mine to carry
I hadn't considered it until now

nodes.text: When you thought about your colleague or team today — what did that change about how you acted?
options: I handled something differently because of them
It made me want to communicate more clearly
It made me worry more — I felt responsible
It gave me perspective on my own problems

nodes.text: When the person you're ultimately serving came to mind — the user, the customer, the stakeholder — what did that do to your energy?
options: It grounded me — reminded me why this matters
It added pressure — the stakes felt higher
It motivated me to do better than I otherwise would have
It shifted my frustration into something more useful

options.condition: answer=Probably yes, but I didn't have bandwidth to notice|I hadn't considered it until now
answer=Yes — and I did check in with them|I don't think so — this was mine to carry
always
always

nodes.text: There's no shame in a day that was all about survival. But here's something worth sitting with: the people around you were in your orbit today too. Bandwidth is real — so is its opposite. Tomorrow, one small act of noticing someone else changes the texture of the day.

You were in your own experience today, and you were honest about that. The fact that you're naming it is the beginning of widening the frame. Even self-awareness about self-centrism is a kind of perspective-taking.
You didn't just experience today — you experienced it *with* people. That shift from 'my day' to 'our day' is exactly what turns individuals into teams. It's easy to forget. You didn't.

When the person you're serving enters your frame, the work changes. It's no longer just a task — it's a contribution to something. Maslow called this self-transcendence: the most reliable source of meaning humans have. You touched that today.

Today's reflection: You described the day as '{A1_OPEN.answer}'. On agency, you leaned {axis1.dominant} — {axis1.summary}. On contribution, you showed {axis2.dominant} — {axis2.summary}. On radius, your frame was {axis3.dominant} — {axis3.summary}. That's today. Tomorrow is unwritten.

axis1.internal: you recognised where your choices lived, even on a hard day
axis1.external: the day's forces felt loud — but you're here, which means you navigated anyway

axis2.contribution: you gave something beyond what was asked
axis2.entitlement: you wanted recognition — which means you cared enough to have standards

axis3.self: your focus was close-in — sometimes that's exactly right
axis3.collective: you held the team in your awareness, not just yourself
axis3.transcendent: you connected today's work to someone it ultimately serves

nodes.text: Rest well. The fact that you reflected means tomorrow you'll begin slightly differently.

routing_rules.note: Decision nodes route based on the most recent question's answer. 'always' means unconditional routing. Bridge nodes auto-advance to their target. Reflection and bridge nodes auto-advance after 2 seconds or user clicks Continue.
routing_rules.axis_dominance: At summary time, axis1.dominant = 'internal' if axis1.internal >= axis1.external, else 'external'. Same logic for axis2 and axis3.
